element and its client-side SVG/PNG export works uniformly (every branch renders a real `<svg>`).
(3) **Vera gains an icon mode** (`generateLoomCard(prompt, 'icon'|'graphic')`): a second system prompt
draws a 24×24 currentColor line mark in the On Air icon language, saved under "Vera icons". The
richer graphic prompt now documents the shared motif vocabulary (frame, person, ripple, rays, check,
flame, cards, devices). (4) **Seed 38 rows** into the root space, sorted into eight categories
(Illustration kit, Lead funnel, Onboarding, On Air, On Air icons, Spot art, Circle templates,
Textures) — 55 code-drawn elements total.

**Consequences.** Every reusable house-style graphic/icon is now catalogued, searchable, sorted, and
exportable in Loom Studio, and Vera can draw both graphics and icons. Data-driven visuals (charts,
the frequency-signature radar, season/breath gauges, mockup frames, one-off UI marks) are
deliberately **not** catalogued — they are dynamic components, not library assets. Rejected: baking
static SVG strings for each set (would duplicate + drift from the source components); a separate
icon table (the polymorphic `registry` field on the existing element kind is enough).

## ADR-483: Loom Studio — folder rail (collections), bulk edits, and Vera as an SVG design assistant

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-01). Extends [ADR-480](DECISIONS.md)/[482](DECISIONS.md).

**Context.** Loom Studio was a flat, paginated grid. To become a real asset + design studio it
needs fast organization at scale (folders, grouping), multi-select editing, and in-place design
iteration on the code-drawn graphics.

**Decision.** (1) **Folder rail** (`loom-rail.tsx`): a left rail with All / by **Type** / by
**Category** (smart folders derived from the `category` field) / **Collections** (custom folders on
the already-migrated `library_collections` + `library_collection_items`; an asset can belong to
many). Navigation is URL-driven (`?kind` / `?category` / `?collection`, preserving `q`/`sort`);
collection create/rename/delete run janitor-gated server actions. (2) **Bulk edits**
(`collections-actions.ts` + a select layer in `loom-grid.tsx`): select assets (per-card + select-
page), then add to a collection, set category, add tags, archive, or delete across the whole
selection. `searchLibraryAssets` gained `category` + `collectionId` filters (membership is resolved
to ids, then an `in` filter — reliable with the untyped service-role client). (3) **Vera as a design
assistant** (`editLoomSvg` + `saveElementSvg`): every SVG **element** gets a "Design with Vera" panel
in the detail drawer. It takes the current SVG (the stored `config.svg`, or the live-rendered element
serialized from the DOM), sends it with a natural-language instruction to `completeText` under an
edit prompt that preserves the viewBox and the color APPROACH (DAWN token classes OR currentColor,
never hex), sanitizes the result (allowlist, both ways), previews it, and on save writes it to the
asset's `config.svg`. Because the grid renders `config.svg` before the code registry, an edited
element shows the edit; clearing `config.svg` restores the original.

**Consequences.** The Studio now organizes thousands of assets into folders + collections, edits in
bulk, and iterates on any graphic by conversation — a functional asset + design studio. Editing a
shared registry element writes a static (still token-classed, so theme-aware) SVG onto that row;
non-destructive **version history** (the `library_versions` table) and a per-space fork-on-edit are
the next step (D3/D5), not this slice. Bulk tag/edit loops run per-row for the union merge (bounded
to 500 selected) rather than a single set-based UPDATE — acceptable at this scale, revisit with
generated columns if it grows.

## ADR-484: Vera checks her own SVG edits with vision (render → look → self-correct)

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-01). Extends [ADR-483](DECISIONS.md).

**Context.** The Design-with-Vera editor edited SVG blind (text in, text out). Editing paths without
seeing the result produced graphics that were often worse than the original — the model had no
feedback on what it actually drew.

**Decision.** Give Vera eyes. After an edit (and on demand via "Check her work"), the client
rasterizes the currently rendered SVG to a PNG on a white field (`rasterizeSvgElement`, reusing the
export canvas path with computed-style inlining so token colors bake in) and sends it to
`reviewLoomSvg`, which calls `completeRaw` with a **vision** message: the goal, the SVG code, and the
rendered image. Vera looks at it and either approves ("GOOD: …") or returns a corrected full `<svg>`
(same viewBox, same color approach, allowlist-sanitized). The client applies the correction and shows
her note. One review runs automatically right after each edit (via a `requestAnimationFrame` once the
candidate paints); further passes are manual. Rendering must be client-side (browser canvas), so the
loop is client-driven — the server stays a pure, gated function.

**Consequences.** Vera can see and fix her own mistakes, which is the missing feedback loop; each
edit costs one extra (vision) sonnet call under the existing `loom-illustration` budget. The review
is a single pass, not an unbounded agentic loop — bounded cost, and the operator drives further
iteration. Rejected: server-side rasterization (would add a native SVG renderer dependency, against
ADR-011) and a fully-automatic multi-round refine loop (cost + latency; a manual button is enough).

## ADR-485: Vera edits by look → understand → redraw (not path-patching), with style-fidelity guards

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-01). Refines [ADR-483](DECISIONS.md)/[484](DECISIONS.md).

**Context.** The first design-assistant editor asked Vera to surgically modify SVG path data
("make the smallest change"). Nudging path coordinates blind produced monsters — a "runner" that
read as a flailing giraffe, and "make it human" turned a clean stick figure into a creepy alien.
The model can't reason well about geometry by editing numbers, and it drifts off the minimal house
style by adding realistic detail.

**Decision.** Route edits through Vera's ORIGINAL creation process instead of a diff:
(1) **Look + understand** — the client rasterizes the current render and `editLoomSvg` first makes a
vision call (`REDRAW_PLAN_SYSTEM`) where Vera names what the illustration depicts, part by part, and
plans the change. (2) **Redraw** — a second call (`REDRAW_SYSTEM`, which embeds the shared
`HOUSE_STYLE` vocabulary now factored out of the create prompt) rebuilds the WHOLE illustration fresh
from that understanding, keeping the viewBox, palette, and composition but drawing shapes from clean
geometry rather than copying path data. (3) The existing vision **self-review** still runs after.
All three prompts carry explicit **style-fidelity/anti-alien guards**: match the original's minimal
abstraction, convey action through POSE, and never add faces/fingers/muscles/realistic detail —
"when unsure, draw less."

**Consequences.** Edits stay in the flat, minimal kit style and read as the intended subject far more
reliably. Cost per edit rises to ~3 sonnet calls (plan + redraw + review), acceptable for a janitor
design tool under the `loom-illustration` budget. The plan step needs the rendered image, so it is
skipped (redraw-only) when rasterization is unavailable. Rejected: extended-thinking on a single call
(config constraints; the explicit plan call is clearer and lets us keep the vision context), and
keeping the surgical-patch editor (it was the root cause of the drift).

## ADR-486: Vera edit modes (Tweak vs Redraw) + the induction "design vibe" for new art

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-01). Refines [ADR-485](DECISIONS.md).

**Context.** Two problems surfaced from use. (1) The single edit path always **redrew** the whole
graphic, so a small refinement ("thin the stroke, make the center leaf translucent") on a clean
monochrome lotus came back as a fully-redrawn, recolored (amber) icon — the redraw prompt also
embedded the create-time house style, which imposed the warm palette onto edits, and the auto-review
over-corrected. (2) The owner wants new art to follow the beta-induction render aesthetic (flat,
warm, filled, amber-led, product-UI feel).

**Decision.** (1) **Two edit modes.** `editLoomSvg(..., mode)`: **Tweak** (default) is a single
surgical call (`TWEAK_SYSTEM`) that changes only what's asked and keeps everything else identical;
**Redraw** keeps the look→plan→redraw flow for bigger changes. Both share `EDIT_PRINCIPLES` which
**preserve the original's exact colors + style** (currentColor stays currentColor; token classes stay
the same) — the create-vibe is NEVER imposed on an edit. The redraw prompt no longer embeds the warm
house style. The **auto-review is conservative** (only fixes clear breakage, never recolors/restyles)
and now runs automatically **only for Redraw**; Tweak checks on demand. The drawer exposes **Tweak /
Redraw** buttons. (2) **Design vibe.** A new spec [`docs/LOOM-DESIGN-LANGUAGE.md`](LOOM-DESIGN-LANGUAGE.md)
distills the induction renders' aesthetic (flat filled shapes, amber-led warm DAWN palette, screen/
card motifs) — wired into the **create** prompts (`GRAPHIC_SYSTEM`, `ICON_SYSTEM`; icons shift from
line-art to warm filled duotone). It steers NEW output only; existing art and edits are untouched.

**Consequences.** Small edits stay faithful (near-identical, same colors); big changes still get a
clean rebuild; new creations carry the warm induction feel. The induction renders themselves are
hand-coded (ADR-068, no prompts existed) — the vibe was reconstructed from the code into the spec.
Rejected: one universal edit prompt (the root cause), and restyling the existing icon kit (the owner
chose non-destructive: new output only).

## ADR-487: The Loom semantic search (Phase 1) — reuse the gte-small text embeddings

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-01). Implements [RESEARCH-ASSET-GEN.md](RESEARCH-ASSET-GEN.md) Phase 1.

**Context.** Loom Studio search was substring/FTS only. We want meaning-based sort + "find similar"
for smart grouping — without new infrastructure. The `library_assets.embedding vector(384)` column
was already reserved (ADR-478), and the platform already ships a key-free, self-hosted gte-small
`embed` edge function (`lib/ai/embed.ts`, ADR-067) used by help + room search.

**Decision.** Reuse that exact pipeline for the Loom. Embed each asset's TEXT (title + description +
category + tags + kind) via `embedText()` into the reserved column, gated by a content hash
(`embedding_hash`) so unchanged assets are skipped; a nightly `embed-library` cron keeps it in sync
(mirrors `embed-help`). Two SECURITY-DEFINER RPCs — `match_library_assets` (query → nearest, space +
kind scoped) and `similar_library_assets` (asset → neighbours) — over an HNSW index. UI: a
**"Most relevant"** sort and a drawer **"Find similar"** (`?similar=<id>`). Both degrade to the
keyword path when AI is off or nothing is embedded yet.

**Consequences.** Semantic sort/grouping ship with **zero new infra, no GPU, no vendor** — 100%
self-hosted. This is *text/metadata* similarity; **visual** similarity (CLIP image embeddings, 512-d,
a separate column) is the Phase 2 fast-follow. Vector generation (OmniSVG/StarVector) and the raster
lane (FLUX) remain later phases pending GPU infra + the follow-up research. Backfill happens on the
first cron run post-deploy (or a manual authorized GET of the endpoint).

## ADR-488: The Loom image studio — Recraft generation + non-destructive editing + version history

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-01). Implements [RESEARCH-ASSET-GEN.md](RESEARCH-ASSET-GEN.md).

**Context.** The Loom generated and edited art by having Claude write inline SVG code, then re-prompting
to refine it (ADR-485/486). Research and use both confirmed a hard ceiling: an LLM hand-writing SVG
can't produce consistent icon SETS or polished raster rewards/trophies, and edits drift after a few
rounds. The owner asked for a fully capable, non-destructive image editor to build on — generate icon
sets + rewards/trophies/cards, edit existing assets, and keep version history with easy rollback. The
research recommended a hybrid: a **managed vector-native engine** for clean icon sets + brand styles,
plus raster for richer art, deferring self-hosted open models (OmniSVG/FLUX) until GPU infra exists.

**Decision.** Adopt **Recraft** as the Loom's managed image/vector engine, behind a thin server-only
client (`lib/loom/recraft.ts`) and janitor + budget-gated server actions
(`app/(main)/admin/library/recraft-actions.ts`). Two lanes: **vector** (`vector_illustration` →
icons/SVG) and **raster** (`digital_illustration` → trophies/rewards/art). Operators can **generate**
(prompt + lane + count → stored `library_assets`), and **edit** a file-backed asset in place with
**Vectorize**, **Remove BG**, and **Variation** (image-to-image). Editing is **non-destructive**: every
edit first snapshots the asset's current state into `library_versions` via a new versioning backbone
(`lib/library/versions.ts` — `recordVersion` / `listVersions` / `rollbackToVersion`, storing the full
`AssetSnapshot` in the version's `recipe` jsonb), then overwrites the live row. The drawer shows a
**Versions** list with one-click **Restore** (which itself snapshots current first, so rollback is
reversible). The whole feature is **inert unless `RECRAFT_API_KEY` is set** (`recraftConfigured()`
hides the generate panel + edit row); a dedicated `recraft` daily budget cap ($10) with per-call cost
accounting ($0.04 raster / $0.08 vector) via the existing usage ledger. Egress note: Recraft is called
server-side only (Vercel egress is open).

**Consequences.** The Loom gets a real image engine — consistent vector sets, raster rewards/trophies,
and controlled instruction edits — plus true non-destructive history that maps onto the DAM's
`library_versions` table (ADR-480), reusing it beyond Filerobot recipes for any edit source. Cost moves
from Anthropic tokens to Recraft per-image pricing, bounded by the feature cap. The versioning module is
source-agnostic, so future edit paths (Vera SVG saves, self-hosted models) can snapshot through the same
API. Rejected/deferred: self-hosting OmniSVG/StarVector (vector) and FLUX (raster) — better quality
control but needs GPU infra; revisit per the research's phased path. Brand-style consistency
(`createStyle` from reference images → `style_id`) is wired in the client but not yet surfaced in the UI;
it's the next step for locking a house style across a generated set.

## ADR-489: The Loom brand styles — train a house look for consistent generated sets

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-01). Extends [ADR-488](DECISIONS.md).

**Context.** The image studio (ADR-488) can generate one-off images, but the owner's core need is
consistent SETS — an icon family that looks like one family, trophies in one house look. A raw prompt
drifts between generations. Recraft supports training a reusable **style** from a few reference images
that returns a `style_id`; passing that id to a generation conditions every image on the learned look.
ADR-488 wired `createStyle()` in the client but left it unsurfaced.

**Decision.** Surface style training end to end. A new `library_styles` table (migration
`20260922000000_library_styles.sql`; service-role/fail-closed like the rest of the Loom) persists each
trained style's `recraft_style_id` + friendly name + lane (the reference images stay on Recraft).
Operators **train a style from existing assets**: select 1–5 images in the grid → **"Train style"** in
the selection bar → name it + pick the lane → `createBrandStyle` downloads the references, calls
Recraft `/styles`, and saves the row. The generate panel gains a **Style** picker (filtered to the
active lane); picking one threads `styleId` into `generateWithRecraft`, which resolves it to the
Recraft id (space-scoped, so a style can't leak across spaces) and conditions the whole set.
`lib/library/styles.ts` holds the data layer (`listStyles`/`recordStyle`/`resolveStyleId`/`deleteStyle`).
Cost is billed to the existing `recraft` feature cap.

**Consequences.** "Generate a matching set" becomes real: train once from a few on-brand images, then
every future generation with that style selected stays in the family. Reference images are reused from
the Loom itself (no separate upload), so the house style compounds as the library grows. Styles are
per-space and forgettable (deleting our pointer doesn't touch Recraft). Deferred: auto-suggesting a
style from a collection, and image-to-image edits conditioned on a style (edits still use the base lane
style today).

## ADR-490: The Loom's unified Create panel — one smart wizard over two engines

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-01). Consolidates [ADR-486](DECISIONS.md) (Create with Vera) +
[ADR-488](DECISIONS.md)/[ADR-489](DECISIONS.md) (Image Studio).

**Context.** The Loom grew two separate create surfaces stacked on the page: **"Create with Vera"**
(an LLM drawing house-style line SVG — a graphic/icon mode) and **"Generate with the Image Studio"**
(Recraft vector/raster generation + brand styles). Two panels, two mental models, overlapping jobs —
the operator had to know which engine to reach for. The owner asked to condense them into one compact,
smart system with smart prompts.

**Decision.** Replace both panels with a single `CreateStudio` component keyed on **what you're
making**, not which engine. Six type chips — **Icon · Spot art · Illustration · Trophy/reward · Card ·
Texture** — each carry a config (engine default, Studio lane, placeholder, and a set of house-voice
**smart-prompt** chips that one-tap fill the box). The engine is chosen automatically and only exposed
where there's a real choice: Icon/Spot art show a **Quick (Vera) / Rich (Studio)** toggle (Vera is the
instant, no-cost default); Illustration/Trophy/Card/Texture always route to the Studio. Vera keeps its
preview→title→save step; the Studio auto-saves and refreshes. Studio-only types are disabled (with a
hint) when `RECRAFT_API_KEY` is unset, so the panel degrades gracefully to just the Vera types. The
trained-brand-style picker (ADR-489) folds into the Studio path's controls. `vera-wizard.tsx` and the
old `RecraftPanel` are deleted; the underlying actions (`generateLoomCard`/`saveLoomCard`,
`generateWithRecraft`, brand styles) are unchanged.

**Consequences.** One surface, one decision for the operator ("what am I making?"), with the
engine tradeoff (instant/free line vs paid/rich generation) surfaced only where it matters. Smart
prompts lower the blank-page cost and keep output on-brand. The panel is compact (a single collapsible
`<details>`) and has no dead "which version do I use" ambiguity. Adding a new output type is a one-row
change to the `TYPES` map. Rejected: a fully automatic engine pick with no toggle (operators want cost
control on the cheap icon path), and keeping the two panels (the explicit ask was to unify).

## ADR-491: Retire the type-driven template system — the Space profile is operator-composed feature-block pages

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-01). Final structural phase (Phase 5) of the Space profile redesign; supersedes the template parts of ADR-472 / ADR-476 / ADR-479.

**Context.** The public Space profile was type-driven: a per-`spaces.type` `RoleBlueprint` (`lib/spaces/blueprints.ts`) declared tabs / primary CTA / hero stats / default accent, and a four-way layout template registry (`lib/spaces/templates.ts`, `SpaceTemplate` = Book / Schedule / Storefront / Hub) re-framed that blueprint and seeded four per-variant Puck presets (`generateSpacePreset*`, `spacePuckData`). Phases 1-4 replaced that render + editor with an **operator-composed multi-page feature-block model**: a Space profile is now a set of freely-arranged Puck feature-block pages (`preferences.pages` + `preferences.pageDocs`), each seeded from ONE universal default (`generateDefaultSpacePage`) and resolved per page (`lib/spaces/profile-pages.ts`). By the end of Phase 4 the profile chrome, the render (`space-landing.tsx`), and the page-scoped editor no longer read blueprints or templates; the picker that drove `preferences.template` was removed. The template system was dead weight with a handful of stray consumers left threading its types.

**Decision.** Delete the type-driven template system and re-home the few defaults it still supplied:
- **Delete** `lib/spaces/blueprints.ts` (+ `blueprintForType` / `provisionableTypes` / `tabForSegment` / `RoleBlueprint` / `EntityTab*` / `HeroStat`), `lib/spaces/templates.ts` (+ `SpaceTemplate` / `SPACE_TEMPLATES` / `SPACE_TEMPLATE_LABEL` / `templateForSpace` / `templateDescriptor*` / `blueprintForSpace` / `readTemplateOverride` / `isSpaceTemplate` / `TemplateResolverInput`), and the old preset generators in `lib/page-editor/templates/space.ts` (`generateSpacePreset` / `generateSpacePresetForSpace` / `spacePuckData` / `readStoredSpaceDoc` / `SpacePresetInput`). The **only** survivor of `space.ts` is `isRenderableSpaceDoc` (the stored-doc renderability guard the profile-pages resolver + the editor/layout actions still use). Also delete the now-dead `setSpaceLayoutTemplate` action + `nextLayoutPreferences` merge helper (their picker is gone), keeping `setSpaceCoverSize` / `readCoverSize` / `setSpaceAccent`.
- **Re-home each survivor.** The per-type DEFAULTS that still matter live in `lib/spaces/profile-config.ts` (pure, self-contained): **accent** → `defaultAccentForType`; **primary-CTA label** → `defaultPrimaryCtaLabel` (`entity-cta.tsx` reads it; it still branches by `space.type` for the transactional surface); **hero stat set** → `defaultHeroStats` (a single UNIVERSAL order, no per-template framing; `resolveProfileStats` now takes just a `spaceId` and drops any zero stat). The **provisionable-types registry** (`provisionableTypes` / `isProvisionableType`) + the **default DAWN skin** (`DEFAULT_SPACE_SKIN`) also move to `profile-config.ts`; the create wizard's provisioning gate (`provision.ts`), the staff view-as selector (`entity-roles.ts`), and the admin per-type defaults grid read them, so the create-a-space flow still lists the same seven types with the same labels. The `ModeProfile` (`lib/spaces/modes.ts`) dropped its unused `blueprint` field (nothing consumed it); type badge labels come from the existing `spaceTypeLabel`.
- **Nav / presets / render** are already the operator model: page nav = `preferences.pages`; every page/reset seeds from `generateDefaultSpacePage`; the render + editor resolve through `resolveSpacePageDoc`.

**Consequences.** One source of truth for a profile's shape (operator-composed blocks), and one source of truth for the small per-type defaults (`profile-config.ts`), instead of two parallel type-driven registries. Two whole modules + their tests are gone; `space.ts` shrinks to a single guard. The public profile no longer varies by a hidden `(type, variant) → template` map, so what an operator arranges is exactly what renders. Behavior is preserved: same provisionable types + labels, same per-type accent/CTA defaults, same honest zero-drop stats, same DAWN skin on new Spaces. Test intents that the blueprint/template tests locked (each core role a distinct accent; unknown type fails closed; lab + partner provisionable; the renderability guard) move to `profile-config.test.ts` + `space.test.ts`. No schema change (`preferences.template` simply stops being read).

## ADR-492: The profile menu is derived section anchors, not stored nav — plus the solid-card block redesign

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-02). Follow-up to ADR-491 (feature-block profile), from operator feedback on the shipped redesign.

**Context.** After ADR-491, a Space's menu row showed only `preferences.pages` (Home + custom sub-pages), so a default profile's menu was a lone "Home" and the page's sections were invisible from the nav. The block kit also read washed-out (hairline translucent cards on a near-identical canvas), editor placeholder stubs leaked onto the public page (a visitor saw a dashed "Contact — add an address…" box), the default About body shipped fill-me-in instructions publicly, and in the Hero cover the action buttons stacked under (and collided with) the avatar row.

**Decision.**
- **Derived anchor menu.** The profile menu is now Home + one ANCHOR link per Home section (`/spaces/x#offerings`) + custom pages. The anchors are DERIVED from the Home doc's blocks (`lib/spaces/section-anchors.ts`: `SECTION_ANCHORS` type→{anchor,label}, `deriveSectionNav`), never stored, so rearranging blocks re-derives the menu for free. **Honest menu:** an anchor is included only when its section will actually render — authored sections are judged from their own props, live sections from `getSpaceSectionPresence` (content-data). Blocks render inside `<section id>` wrappers (`scroll-mt` for the sticky header, `empty:hidden` so an honest-empty render leaves no phantom gap).
- **One request-cached live read.** `getSpaceLiveContent` (React.cache, keyed spaceId+slug) backs BOTH the chrome's presence flags and the page body's block metadata, so the menu and the page can never disagree and the queries run once per request. `getSpaceContentData` no longer gates the live reads on `input` (the staff preview now matches the public render exactly).
- **Operator admin links in the menu.** Managers see right-aligned `Manage` + (console types) `CRM` past a hairline in the same menu row; visitors never do.
- **Editor-only placeholders.** Authored blocks (About / Offerings / Contact / Quick links / Team) gate their dashed stubs on `puck.isEditing`; the live page renders nothing when unfilled. The default About body seeds EMPTY (the heading keeps the brand name).
- **Solid-card redesign.** `InfoCard` and the stat tiles move from hairline translucent (`border-border/60 bg-surface/60`, no shadow) to solid (`rounded-2xl border-border bg-surface shadow-sm`, `p-6 sm:p-8`); section titles step up (`text-xl sm:text-2xl tracking-tight`, `mb-6`); the SpaceLayout rhythm becomes main `space-y-14` / side rail `space-y-6` / grid `gap-10 lg:gap-14`; the Space content blocks (Updates / Reviews / FAQ) drop marketing display type for the profile title grammar. Both cover sizes render ONE identity row: avatar + name left, action buttons pushed right on the same line.

**Consequences.** A new Space's menu is pre-populated with its real sections and stays truthful as data appears (Book shows once availability exists, Reviews once the first review lands). No stored nav format to migrate; `preferences.pages` is unchanged and custom pages still append. Visitors never see editor scaffolding. The old always-on `SpaceEvents` empty-state stub (which rendered on live pages with no events) is fixed by the same `isEditing` gate.

## ADR-493: Plan the migration off the deprecated `@measured/puck` — own the block layer in-house, keep the persisted `Data` shape

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-02). Planning decision; execution phased. Full plan: [PUCK-MIGRATION-PLAN.md](PUCK-MIGRATION-PLAN.md).

**Context.** The visual page editor is built on `@measured/puck` (pinned `^0.20.2` in `package.json`), which is flagged deprecated / no longer actively maintained upstream. The package powers three operator surfaces off ONE shared block registry (`lib/page-editor/config.tsx`): the marketing pages, the Space profile pages, and the member Spotlight. Its runtime footprint is narrower than it looks: the load-bearing usage is the public `<Render>` (`@measured/puck/rsc` on every marketing route + `app/(main)/circles`, `components/spaces/space-landing.tsx`, `components/spotlight/puck-render.tsx`; the client `<Render>` in the mobile + Spotlight previews) and the desktop `<Puck>` canvas (`components/page-editor/editor.tsx`, `components/spotlight/puck-editor.tsx`, `components/spaces/space-landing-editor.tsx`). Everything else is either type-only (`Data`/`Config`/`ComponentConfig` across ~40 files) or already ours: the ~68 blocks are plain in-repo `ComponentConfig`s, and the mobile editor (`components/page-editor/mobile/*`) already reads/writes the exact Puck `Data` shape WITHOUT the runtime (`field-form.tsx` reimplements field rendering; `data-ops.ts` reimplements document ops). Crucially, the persisted documents are our own serialized `Data` (`{ content, root }`): marketing pages in `pages.data`/`published_data`, Space pages in `spaces.preferences.pageDocs[*]` (+ legacy `preferences.puck`), and Spotlight via the migration-free bridge `lib/spotlight/puck/convert.ts`. Staying on a frozen deprecated dependency risks unpatched security issues on public traffic and React 19 / Next 16 / Tailwind v4 compat drift with no upstream fix.

**Decision.** Plan a staged exit onto an in-house block layer, keeping the `Data` + `config` contract byte-for-byte so NO stored document is migrated. (0) Pin to an exact version immediately. (1) Re-home the type imports behind `lib/page-editor/types.ts`. (2) Replace `<Render>` with a small, pure in-house `<BlockRender>` over the existing registry (walks `content`, looks up `config.components[type].render`, threads `metadata`, recurses into the `slot` regions used by `Container`/`Columns`/`SpaceLayout`), guarded by golden-markup parity tests and rolled out per surface behind a flag — removing the package from all public traffic first. (3) Replace the desktop `<Puck>` canvas by promoting the mobile editor's Puck-free core and adding slot drag-and-drop, keeping `<Puck>` behind a flag until parity. (4) Delete the dependency. Reject a wholesale swap to another editor (Plasmic/Builder/GrapesJS/etc.): every alternative uses a different document model, forcing a one-way migration of every live doc for no gain, trading one vendor for another.

**Consequences.** The vendor risk is retired without a data migration or a big-bang cutover; each phase is independently shippable and reversible by a flag flip or revert (no destructive DB step). The block registry, the DAWN token styling, and the save/publish actions (`publishPage`, `publishSpaceLanding`/`resetSpaceLanding`, `saveSpotlightDraft`/`publishSpotlightDraft`) are unchanged. We take on ownership of the renderer (small, pure) and, in Phase 3, the desktop authoring UX including nested-slot drag-and-drop (the one genuinely net-new piece). Going forward, new docs stamp a `schemaVersion` (additive) so a future shape change is detectable; the existing `isRenderable`/`isRenderableSpaceDoc` guards already degrade an unrenderable doc to the coded fallback rather than throwing. Estimated effort: pin ~1h, type surface ~1d, renderer ~3–5d, desktop editor parity ~2–3wk, removal ~0.5d (~4–6 weeks total, phaseable).

## ADR-494: Guard Stripe membership webhooks with a per-profile event-ordering timestamp, not a live re-fetch

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-02). Shipped: migration `20261003000000_membership_event_ordering_guard`, `app/api/stripe/webhook/route.ts`. Meta-scan Phase (Stripe event ordering).

**Context.** Stripe does not guarantee webhook delivery order. The member Crew/Supporter path (`setTier` in `app/api/stripe/webhook/route.ts`) wrote `profiles.membership_tier` unconditionally on every event, so a delayed `customer.subscription.updated(status=active)` that Stripe *created* before a `customer.subscription.deleted` but *delivered* after it would re-grant a canceled tier — the member keeps paid access for free. `checkout.session.completed` has the same hazard (a very delayed original-signup event landing after a later cancel). Two fixes were considered: (A) stamp each member profile with the `event.created` of the last APPLIED billing event and ignore any event created at-or-before it; (B) re-fetch the live subscription from Stripe inside the webhook before writing.

**Decision.** Option A. Add `profiles.last_stripe_event_at` and a `SECURITY DEFINER` RPC `apply_membership_event_atomic(_profile, _event_at, _tier, _payment_status, _is_supporter, _customer_id)` that reads the stamp, applies the write only when `_event_at > last_stripe_event_at` (**strict** `>`), and advances the stamp — all under a per-profile `pg_advisory_xact_lock`, so two concurrent deliveries for the same member cannot both pass the check and race the write. The RPC is `service_role`-only (revoked from `public/anon/authenticated`) and mirrors the repo's existing atomic-RPC idiom (`award_gems_atomic`, `reserve_ticket_atomic`, `claim_outbox_jobs`). `setTier` routes through it carrying `event.created`; a stale event returns `{ applied:false }` (no error, acked 200), only a real DB error throws so the outer catch releases the idempotency claim and 500s for a Stripe retry. Reject B: it adds a synchronous `stripe.subscriptions.retrieve` (new latency + failure + rate-limit surface, and retrieving a just-deleted subscription is awkward) and STILL needs the same advisory-lock compare-and-set to be concurrency-safe — so B is A plus an external call. A strictly dominates.

**Consequences.** The named bug closes: a cancel stamps a newer `last_stripe_event_at`, and the out-of-order active is rejected while tier stays free; `checkout.session.completed` is now guarded too. Per-profile is a valid per-subscription key (a member holds at most one personal Crew/Supporter sub, `event.created` is globally monotonic so a resubscribe's created is later than the prior cancel — no false lockout, and Space subscription events never reach this path). Intentional edge: strict `>` means two distinct events sharing the same one-second `event.created` delivered out of order resolve last-restrictive-wins only in the safe direction (a same-second active after a cancel is dropped; a dropped same-second cancel self-corrects on the next event and is far less costly than silent free access) — real out-of-order deliveries are seconds-to-minutes apart, so ties are near-nonexistent. The column is additive and inert while `billing_live` is OFF; RLS is unchanged; the RPC is reached through a localized method cast until `lib/database.types.ts` regenerates (ADR-246). Behavior verified by `supabase/tests/membership_event_ordering_guard.test.sql` (pgTAP) + `app/api/stripe/webhook/route.test.ts` (wiring).

## ADR-495: The Crew Supporter badge charges a one-time PWYW contribution into a `supporter_contributions` ledger, dormant behind `billing_live`

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-02). Shipped: migration `20261005000000_supporter_contributions`, `lib/billing/supporter.ts`, `app/(main)/upgrade/actions.ts` (`startSupporterContribution` / `confirmSupporterContribution`), `app/api/webhooks/stripe/route.ts`. Follow-up to ADR-463 (Supporter retired as a tier -> PWYW badge on Crew).

**Context.** ADR-463 retired Supporter as a membership tier and made it an opt-in pay-what-you-want (PWYW) BADGE on Crew (`profiles.is_supporter`). Phase C shipped only the badge FLAG write (`toggleSupporterBadge`); the actual contribution CHARGE and its ledger were deferred with a note in `app/(main)/upgrade/actions.ts`. This ADR builds them. Two shapes were considered: (A) a ONE-TIME direct-platform charge per contribution, recorded as a discrete ledger row (mirrors the Founders Round + `tips` pattern); (B) a monthly RECURRING PWYW subscription, reconciled on `invoice.paid` like membership dues.

**Decision.** Option A. A supporter contribution is a ONE-TIME Stripe Checkout charge (`mode:'payment'`) at the member-chosen amount, gated behind `billingLive()` at the action call site. The domain lives in `lib/billing/supporter.ts`: `startSupporterContribution` validates the amount against the operator PWYW floor (`catalog.pwyw.minCents`) + a `$1,000` ceiling, creates a `kind:'supporter_contribution'` session, and inserts a `pending` `supporter_contributions` row (refusing the URL + expiring the session if the row never lands, exactly like `createTipCheckout`). `recordSupporterContributionFromSession` is the SINGLE grant point shared by the Stripe webhook branch and the success-page confirm action: it flips the row `pending -> succeeded` (idempotent, keyed on the checkout session id), books ONE `donation` on the entity-partitioned ledger for the Foundation (the full amount — a direct platform payment, NOT a Connect destination charge like tips), and turns `is_supporter` on. A new `supporter_contributions` table mirrors the `tips` RLS posture (member reads own; service-role writes only). Reject B: a recurring PWYW subscription needs its own subscription reconciliation AND a `kind` guard in `recordMembershipDuesFromInvoice` to avoid double-counting a contribution as membership dues; a one-time charge per contribution is the smaller, self-contained surface and the `supporter_contributions` name already reads as a ledger of discrete gifts. Recurring can be layered later as its own channel.

**The dormant invariant.** While `billing_live` is OFF, `startSupporterContribution` NEVER touches Stripe: it turns the badge on (`is_supporter = true`) and returns `{ dormant:true }`, byte-identical to today's `toggleSupporterBadge` behavior (no session, no card, no ledger row). The charge path only runs once `billingLive()` (Stripe env keys AND the master `billing_live` flag) is true. `recordSupporterContributionFromSession` does not itself re-check the flag: it is only ever reached AFTER a real paid Stripe session exists, so a paid contribution is honored even if an operator later toggles the flag (mirrors `grantFounderFromSession`).

**Consequences.** The deferred charge + ledger are now built and inert; flipping `billing_live` on (after the Stripe env + a `checkout.session.completed` webhook are configured) activates them with zero further code change. The table is additive; RLS is unchanged elsewhere; the ledger reuses the existing `recordFinancialTransaction` front door (idempotent per contribution). `supporter_contributions` is not yet in `lib/database.types.ts`, so `lib/billing/supporter.ts` reaches it through a localized untyped view (ADR-246) until `gen types` runs. Behavior locked by `lib/billing/supporter.test.ts` (validation floor/ceiling, the idempotent grant + Foundation donation + badge flip, the no-op guards) and `app/(main)/upgrade/actions.test.ts` (the dormant-when-OFF no-charge invariant + the live session shape). NEEDS LIVE CONFIG to fully activate: the Stripe env keys + the `checkout.session.completed` webhook endpoint, then flipping the `billing_live` platform flag — none attempted here.
## ADR-496: Prod migrations are applied via MCP `apply_migration` only, never `supabase db push`; the repo files are the authored source, the applied set is prod truth

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-02). Meta-scan Phase (#16, migration stamp reconciliation).

**Context.** The prod project ("Frequency Community", `azsqfeonabsbmemvddqd`) has been migrated for months via the Supabase MCP `apply_migration` tool, which stamps `supabase_migrations.schema_migrations.version` with an **apply-time wall-clock timestamp** (e.g. `20260630180444`, `20260603035927`) and records the human name (short, or occasionally the full intended filename) in the `name` column. The repo, meanwhile, names its files with **logical, rounded** stamps (`20260915000000_pricing_plan_collapse.sql`, `20260603000001_demo_0_infrastructure.sql`). These two stamping schemes diverged across essentially the whole post-2024 history: a precise diff (repo file stamp vs the set of applied `version`s) found **311 of 408 repo files carry a stamp that is NOT an applied `version`**. `supabase db push` decides what to run by diffing repo filename stamps against `schema_migrations.version`, so a `db push` against prod would treat those 311 files as *unapplied* and try to **re-run** them. Several are destructive to re-run, most sharply `20260915000000_pricing_plan_collapse.sql` (it re-partitions `spaces.entitlements` and remaps `spaces.plan`; a second run mangles the billing namespace). A handful of migrations are even double-recorded (once under an apply-time stamp with the full filename as `name`, once under the correct rounded stamp), e.g. `advisor_sweep_initplan_fk_indexes` and `challenge_streak_atomic`.

Two reconciliations were on the table. **(A) Rename each repo file to its applied stamp** so `db push` would see them as applied. **(B) Formally adopt "MCP-apply-only, never `db push`"** and document that the repo files are the authored source while the applied set is prod truth.

**Decision.** Option B. Formally adopt the workflow that is already in force:

- **Prod schema changes are applied ONLY via the Supabase MCP `apply_migration` tool.** `supabase db push` is **never** run against prod.
- **The repo `supabase/migrations/*.sql` files are the authored SOURCE** (code review, the pgTAP fresh-apply / `db-tests` path, and local/branch databases that ARE built with `db push` on a clean slate all consume them in filename order).
- **The applied set in prod `schema_migrations` is prod TRUTH.** Its `version` stamps are apply-time and deliberately NOT expected to match repo filenames. Divergence between the two is normal and is NOT drift to "fix" by pushing.
- **New forward migrations still ship as repo files** with a monotonic logical stamp later than every existing file (this ADR ships `20261006000000_drop_redundant_space_subscription_items_index.sql`), and are applied to prod with `apply_migration`.

Reject A (rename to applied stamps): it would rewrite 300+ filenames, reorder the local-dev / fresh-apply sequence (apply-time stamps interleave differently from the authored logical order, so a rename risks changing the order in which a clean DB builds and thus the schema it produces), and buy nothing, since we do not `db push` prod anyway. The safe, non-destructive move is to document the boundary, not to churn history. (Targeted single-file renames were considered and rejected for the same reordering risk; none was clearly safe in isolation.)

**Consequences.** No file is renamed, no migration is re-run, no destructive SQL touches prod. The one real hazard (someone runs `db push` and re-applies `pricing_plan_collapse` et al.) is closed by making the rule explicit in three places: `docs/DATABASE.md` §"Working with migrations" (the `db push # apply` line is replaced with the MCP-apply-only flow + an explicit warning), the PR template (which already carries "Do NOT `supabase db push`"), and this ADR. Local and CI ephemeral databases still build from the repo files with `db push` on a clean slate (there is no prod history to collide with there, so that use is safe and unchanged). Accepted residue: prod `schema_migrations` keeps a few duplicate rows (a migration recorded under both an apply-time and a logical stamp) and apply-time stamps that read out of authored order; both are cosmetic in the history table and carry no runtime effect, so they are left as-is rather than mutated. If we ever need `db push` parity (e.g. a future migration tool that only reads filenames), the correct move is a one-time `supabase migration repair --status applied` reconciliation performed deliberately, not an ad-hoc rename.

## ADR-497: Dependency-hygiene pass — patch/minor bumps only, `postcss` deduped to the patched build via a `pnpm.overrides` pin, majors held for Dependabot

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-02). Meta-scan Phase B (dependency hygiene).

**Context.** The meta-scan roadmap Phase B called for bumping patch/minor dependencies, holding majors, and re-running `pnpm audit`. A read-only survey (`pnpm outdated`, `pnpm audit`, `pnpm ls`) found the tree already very current: of the called-out packages (resend, stripe, `@supabase/supabase-js`, lucide-react, tailwindcss, `@sentry/nextjs`, `@anthropic-ai/sdk`, supabase CLI) **every one was already at latest**. Only two packages were behind, both **exact-pinned** (so a plain `pnpm update` would not move them): `next` 16.2.9 → **16.2.10** (a patch within minor 16.2; no 16.3/17 exists) and `eslint-config-next`, which must stay in lockstep with `next`. Audit was down to **1 moderate**: `postcss@8.4.31` (GHSA-qx2v-qp2m-jg93, XSS when re-stringifying untrusted CSS into an HTML `<style>` — not a path Next exercises at build time), pulled transitively and pinned by `next`. The previously-tracked second vuln (`uuid`, via `@measured/puck`) was **already gone** — the Puck migration (ADR-493, #1435) removed the only source, and the lockfile now has zero `uuid`/`puck` references. Two majors were available and out of scope: `@types/node` → 26, `eslint` → 10 (flat-config/API-breaking).

Three options for the lone `postcss` finding were weighed. **(A)** wait for a future `next` release to bump its pinned `postcss` (out of our hands, leaves the finding open indefinitely). **(B)** a `pnpm.overrides` entry forcing `postcss` to the patched line. **(C)** ignore it as low-real-world-risk (fails the "audit ≤ prior, drive to 0" bar and leaves a standing advisory).

**Decision.** Option B, with the override pinned to the **exact version already in the tree**. The patched `postcss@8.5.16` was *already* present (pulled by `@tailwindcss/postcss@4.3.2`), so `"pnpm": { "overrides": { "postcss": "8.5.16" } }` is a pure **dedupe** — it collapses the vulnerable `8.4.31` subtree onto the patched `8.5.16` with **zero new download**, deterministically (an exact pin rather than the range `>=8.5.10`, so the resolved version can't drift on a future install). `next`/`eslint-config-next` were edited to `16.2.10` directly in `package.json` (preserving the exact-pin convention — carets are reserved for non-framework deps) and the lockfile reconciled with `pnpm install`. Majors were **held**: their caret ranges already cap them within the current major, and `.github/dependabot.yml` already ships majors as individual review PRs while batching minor/patch weekly — so a deliberate hold here matches the repo's existing automation posture rather than fighting it.

**Consequences.** `pnpm audit` goes **1 moderate → 0**; `postcss@8.4.31` is gone from the lockfile (only `8.5.16` remains). `next` runs on 16.2.10 — `pnpm build` compiles (Turbopack) and the type pass is clean; the only build step that fails locally is page-data collection, which needs real Supabase env (env-only, Vercel-side, unchanged from prior PRs). All CI `checks` steps pass (tsc, lint, 3,378 tests, `check:authz`, `check:canon`) and `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` is green. The override is the single ongoing maintenance note: once a future `next` ships a `postcss ≥ 8.5.10` pin of its own, the override becomes a no-op and can be removed; until then it is load-bearing for the clean audit. Held majors (`@types/node` 26, `eslint` 10) are left for a deliberate, separately-tested pass — ESLint 10 in particular is a flat-config/API break that the unit suite would not fully catch.

## ADR-498: Gift Gems is open to all members, a spendable-only transfer, and now notifies the recipient

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-02). Final meta-scan — Gift Gems mechanics/policy finalization.

**Context.** Gift Gems (Rewards Economy v3, ADR-305) lets a member spend their own Gems to credit another member. The race-safe backend (`giftGems` → `gift_gems_atomic`) shipped, and the UI was wired in the final meta-scan (#1440). Two things were left open: (1) **who may gift** — the trigger was initially gated behind `canSpend` (Crew) as a conservative default, with a note that the backend is tier-agnostic; and (2) the gift landed **silently** in the recipient's wallet with no notification. This ADR settles both, plus records the economy-integrity properties so gifting can't become a status-buying or farming vector as the community scales.

**Decision.**
- **Open to every member with a spendable balance (`balance > 0`), not just Crew.** Gifting is a generosity act, and the mission runs on members showing up for each other — putting a paywall on generosity works against that. The `giftGems` backend was already deliberately tier-agnostic; this aligns the UI (`components/widgets/vault/vault-store.tsx`) with it by dropping the `canSpend` gate (still `balance > 0`). Note the asymmetry with Vault **cash-in**, which stays a Crew perk: *spending on yourself* is the monetization lever; *giving to someone else* is not.
- **Spendable-only transfer (unchanged, and now explicit policy).** Spendable Gems are conserved: the giver's `gem_gifts` row draws down their spendable balance (`lib/store/balance.ts`), the recipient is credited the same amount. No spendable Gems are minted or destroyed. `lifetime_gems` stays monotonic for the giver ("total ever earned" is never clawed back). The `gift_gems_atomic` RPC remains the authority: it rechecks the balance under a per-giver advisory lock, so two concurrent spends can never overspend, and the action never loses Gems on any failure path.
- **Notify the recipient.** `giftGems` now inserts a best-effort `type:'gift_received'` notification ("{giver} sent you N Gems"). A gift is a *seen* act of connection, not a silent bump. Best-effort by design: a notification failure must never fail or reverse a gift that already committed.

**Economy integrity (why this is safe to open up).** Standing on Frequency is driven by **Zaps** (real-world) and **rank** (Journeys), NOT by Gems — so a gift cannot buy status, leaderboard position, or Amplitude. The only thing a gift moves is *spendable* Gems, whose sinks are cosmetics/titles/badges (and the membership-credit SKU, which is currently deactivated). The farming payoff is therefore low: a ring of sock-puppets grinding daily-capped Gems to funnel to one account gains only cosmetic spend, and only if that account is Crew. Accepted residue: a gift does raise the recipient's `lifetime_gems` (their "earned total", shown transparently in the ledger as a received gift) — honest, and inert for standing since Gems don't feed rank/Amplitude.

**Consequences.** Gifting is reachable by any member with Gems (help: `content/help/membership/the-gem-store.md` "Gifting Gems"; `docs/CHANGELOG.md`), recipients now get a notification, and the money path is unchanged (no migration; `gift_gems_atomic` still guards the balance). **Deferred lever, documented not built:** if farming/wash-trading ever appears, the right response is a **daily gifting cap enforced inside `gift_gems_atomic`** (mirroring the daily Gem-earn caps) — not an account-standing gate, which would tax genuine generosity. Not added now because the current payoff doesn't justify the money-path migration or an invented cap number. `lib/rewards/gifts.test.ts` continues to lock the input-guard contract (self-gift, non-positive/non-integer amount, missing member).

## ADR-499: Verification-gated Zaps — per-task, held-then-released, gated at the Zap ledger

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-02). Completes the deferred member-verification phase (ADR-418).

**Context.** ADR-418 reserved `profiles.verified_at` / `verification_method` as a baseline, explicitly deferring "how a member becomes verified" as a product decision. Meanwhile crew tasks carried a `requires_verification` flag whose Zaps were credited immediately on completion — verification (`approveVerification`) only stamped `verified_by` and never actually gated the reward, so it was a no-op flag over an already-paid Zap. The owner decided the model: **a member must be verified to earn a flagged act's Zaps**, and verification happens by **timer** (finishing a real timed session), **location** (geofenced check-in), **scanned code** (QR/NFC at a gathering), or **leader grant**. Three sub-decisions were taken with the owner: gate scope = **per-task** (only `requires_verification` acts hold their Zaps; everything else pays immediately); held Zaps = **held then released** (they accrue the moment verification lands, nothing lost); "timer" = **completing a timed session** of the required length.

**Decision.** Gate at the **Zap ledger**, not the balance. The current credit model (rewards_economy_v2) is: `after_crew_completion` writes a `zap_transactions` ledger row and a separate trigger derives `current_season_zaps` + rank. So "hold" = withhold the ledger row; "release" = write it. Implementation (`20261009000000_verification_gated_zaps`):

- `crew_completions.verified_at` + `verification_method`; `crew_tasks.verification_method` (`leader|timer|location|code`, null → leader).
- `after_crew_completion` (AFTER INSERT) writes the ledger row **unless** the task requires verification and the row is not yet verified (an auto-method may insert already-verified → credit now).
- `after_crew_completion_verified` (AFTER UPDATE) writes the held ledger row exactly once, on the `verified_at` null → set transition, and only for a `requires_verification` task (so a stray update can't double-pay).
- `lib/crew/verify.ts` `verifyCrewCompletion(completionId, method, verifierId?)` is the **single release point** for all four methods; it stamps `verified_at` idempotently (only still-held rows). `approveVerification` (leader grant) now calls it. Non-verification completions and `logCompletion` are unchanged.

Rejected the direct-balance approach (the pre-v2 trigger incremented `current_season_zaps` + advanced rank in the trigger): it is no longer the model, and reintroducing it caused an incident (below).

**Incident + lesson (recorded deliberately).** The first apply of this migration reconstructed `after_crew_completion` from the **2024** trigger body (direct `current_season_zaps` update + rank advance using the retired `sifu/sensei/…` enum), overwriting the live v2 ledger-insert version. That broke crew crediting in prod for ~5 minutes until a corrective apply restored the ledger model. Root cause: `create or replace` on a shared prod trigger without first reading the **current** function body. Standing rule: before replacing any prod function/trigger, read its live definition (`pg_get_functiondef`) — never reconstruct from an old migration. Verified after the fix with a rolled-back `DO`-block test (held = no credit, release = +N, immediate = +N), run under a `service_role` JWT claim because `prevent_economy_self_edit` exempts only that role.

**Consequences.** Flagged tasks now genuinely hold their Zaps until a leader approves, and the release is a single, idempotent, testable path ready for the auto-methods. **Follow-up (documented, not built):** the timer/location/code auto-methods need a completion entry point that carries proof and a task↔proof link (which timed session / geofence / QR node verifies which task) — none exists in the schema yet, so today every `requires_verification` task is released by the leader grant, and `verification_method` on the task is informational until those entry points land. `crew_completions.verified_at` reaches untyped until `gen types` runs (ADR-246).

## ADR-500: Cross-surface Puck template system — one shared block library, per-surface templates, one content model

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-02). Phase A (foundation), Phase B (additive member bridge), and Phase C (shared resolver + registry completion) all shipped.

**Context.** Four surfaces already render off the ONE in-house block library (`lib/page-editor/config.tsx`, the frozen `Data` shape ADR-493): the marketing **website** (`pages.published_data` → `getPublishedData` → `getTemplate` → legacy), a **space** profile (operator-composed multi-page docs on `spaces.preferences.pageDocs`, seeded from `generateDefaultSpacePage`, rendered by `space-landing.tsx`, ADR-491), and the member **spotlight** (Signal page — `profiles.meta.spotlight` bridged into a Puck doc by `spotlightLayoutToPuck`, rendered by `components/spotlight/puck-render.tsx`, ADR ~Phase 3). Each grew its own presets and resolver ad hoc, and there was no explicit statement that these are variations on ONE architecture, nor a home for the fourth surface — the member's own public page (`people/[handle]`), still bespoke. The owner's principle: **ONE shared block library; per-surface templates that render the SAME content DISTINCTLY; content read from the same store; server-resolved values (live counts, friend faces) injected via Puck `metadata`, never stored.** This ADR formalizes that so "templates across the board" is explicit + extensible, without touching any live render.

**Decision.** Add a typed, documented **surface registry** and the missing member starter, ADDITIVELY (no live page render or editor allowlist changed).

- `lib/page-editor/surfaces.ts` — `SURFACES: Record<SurfaceKey, SurfaceDef>` for the four surfaces (`website | space | spotlight | user`). Each def names its `contentSource` (where content lives), its `blockPreset` (the config.categories it leads with), its `resolver` (how the public render resolves a `Data` doc — documentation, not enforcement), and its `defaultTemplate` (a pure `() => Data`, or `null` when resolved per-slug elsewhere, as website is). PURE (no server/Next imports), so it is safe in the client editor and RSC render alike. `getSurface(key)` throws on a bad key so a typo fails loud.
- `lib/page-editor/templates/profile-default.ts` — `generateDefaultProfilePage(name)`, the member (`user`) starter: an identity-led, link-tree-forward FLAT block list — `SpaceAbout` (identity/About, body seeds empty), `SpotlightStats` (public counts: zaps/streak/gems, a LiveStats-style dynamic block whose values are server-resolved via `metadata.spotlight`, never stored), `LinkTree` (the bio-link list), `TopFriends` (server-resolved faces). Honest at day zero (dynamic blocks render nothing until real data; About seeds empty); composed only from blocks already in `config.components`; DAWN tokens, no em dashes.
- Wiring: `getTemplate('profile')` returns the new member default (registered in the templates map with a neutral name; a live seed passes the member's), and the `user` surface's `defaultTemplate` points at the same generator. `lib/page-editor/surfaces.test.ts` locks the registry honest: every non-null `defaultTemplate` is `isRenderable` (only known block types), and every `blockPreset` entry is a real `config.categories` key — so nothing orphans as surfaces are added.

**Why a registry, not just more presets.** The four surfaces differ only in *content source*, *block emphasis*, and *resolver* — the block library, the `Data` shape, and the `metadata` injection channel are shared. Naming that in one typed place is what makes the architecture extensible (a fifth surface is one `SurfaceDef`) and testable (the preset↔category and template↔block-library links are asserted, not assumed). It is documentation-first on purpose: the resolvers already exist in code, so the registry describes them rather than re-implementing a dispatcher that would risk diverging from the live paths.

**Phased plan.**
- **Phase A (this ADR, done):** the registry, the member starter template + its wiring, and the honesty test. No live render or the editor's `EDITABLE_PAGES` allowlist changed.
- **Phase B (shipped 2026-07-02, owner-approved "additive bridge"):** the member `people/[handle]` page now additively renders the member's **published Spotlight** block body (same source, `profiles.meta.spotlight`) in the in-app profile style, via a self-contained fail-safe Server Component (`components/profile/profile-links-section.tsx`) behind `<Suspense>`. It reuses the existing Spotlight readers + `spotlightLayoutToPuck` converter + `metadata.spotlight` (no new column/migration/storage), drops Spotlight's own identity header/theme (the profile has its own), and returns `null` when there's no published Spotlight or on any error — so the bespoke profile stays the primary render and can never break. Same content, distinct surface.
- **Phase C (shipped 2026-07-02, owner-approved "phase c"):** the cross-surface architecture is completed by DE-DUPLICATING the resolution and naming the deliberate store shape — NOT by a physical table merge. (1) One shared resolver, `lib/spotlight/puck/resolve.ts` (`spotlightRenderMeta` + `spotlightPuckDoc` + `SPOTLIGHT_PUBLIC_BASE`), now backs BOTH surfaces that render a member's Spotlight body — the standalone Signal page (`components/spotlight/puck-render.tsx`, `seedWhenEmpty:true`) and the in-app profile section (`components/profile/profile-links-section.tsx`, `seedWhenEmpty:false`). Phase B had copied the metadata-build + layout→Puck bridge inline into the profile section, so the two could silently drift (a stat added to one surface, forgotten on the other); they are now identical by construction, differing only in chrome + the empty-layout policy. Guarded by `resolve.test.ts`. (2) The surface registry's `spotlight` + `user` resolvers now cite that one function, and `user.contentSource` states plainly it shares `profiles.meta.spotlight` (the Phase B additive bridge) — there is NO separate user store. **The "one store" clause is realized as one LOGICAL content model (one block library, one `Data` shape, one editor shell, one resolver per body) over per-ENTITY physical stores** (`pages` for the site, `spaces.preferences.pageDocs` for a Space, `profiles.meta.spotlight` for a member) — because a member's Spotlight and a marketing page belong to different owners/scopes and must not share a table. A physical merge was considered and REJECTED: it buys nothing (the block model + editor are already shared), needs a risky data migration, and would break the per-entity RLS/ownership boundary. The editor + mobile/display entry points were already shared before Phase C (`ResponsiveEditor` backs the website, Space, and Spotlight editors alike), so no new entry point was needed.

**Consequences.** The cross-surface architecture is now explicit, guarded by a test, and DRY — the same member content resolves identically wherever it renders. A new surface or a new member starter has an obvious, typed home; a new Spotlight stat is added in one resolver and appears on every surface at once. Nothing shipped changes behavior — `surfaces.ts` is inert documentation + a pure `() => Data` per surface until a caller (Phase B/C) reads it. The member page remains bespoke today; `defaultTemplate` for `user` is the seed Phase B will use. `contentSource`/`resolver` strings are notes, not a source of truth — the code + `supabase/migrations/` remain authoritative — so they must be kept in step by hand if a resolver moves.

## ADR-501: The App catalog derives FROM the code registries (code → catalog); LP2's proposed inversion is rejected

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-02). Supersedes the planned "LP2 — invert registries to derive from catalog" (docs/LOOM-PLATFORM.md). No code change; this records the direction LP1 shipped and closes LP2.

**Context.** The Loom Platform plan (docs/LOOM-PLATFORM.md) sketched a Wave-2 task **LP2: "AdminModule / LAYOUT_MODULES / element-catalog become adapters over `APPS`"** — i.e. make the `APPS` catalog the source of truth and have the code registries *derive from it*. When LP1 was actually built, the App contract landed the OTHER way round, on purpose: `lib/apps/catalog.ts` computes `APPS` by `.map()`-ing over the existing code registries — `ADMIN_MODULES` (the editor spine), `LAYOUT_MODULES` (page blocks), and `ELEMENT_SEEDS` (code-drawn SVG) — so `EDITOR_APPS ∪ PAGE_APPS ∪ ELEMENT_APPS = APPS`. The catalog is a read-only *projection* of code, exactly like `lib/library/element-registry.tsx` ("code is source of truth, The Loom indexes it read-only") and `lib/apps/app-registry.tsx` (the Loom Apps lane), and `lib/library/splash-templates.ts` (the splash catalog). This principle is stated verbatim in all three modules.

**Decision.** Keep the derive-direction **code → catalog**. Do not invert. LP2 as originally framed is **rejected**, not deferred.

**Why inverting is wrong here.**
- **It contradicts the platform's own principle.** Every Loom lane is defined as a read-only index over a code source of truth (elements, Apps, splash templates, sequences). Making `APPS` authoritative and the registries derive would make the App lane the one lane that owns its domain instead of indexing it — the exact drift the principle exists to prevent.
- **The catalog is pure metadata; the registries carry the render graph.** `lib/apps/catalog.ts` is intentionally free of component imports so it stays cheap to import and unit-testable (see `lib/apps/app-registry.tsx`: "the pure metadata stays cheap to import; tests can load the registry without pulling the render graph"). The id→component bindings live in `lib/apps/bindings.ts` / `lib/widgets/registry.tsx`. If `APPS` became the source, it would have to carry (or re-export) component references, coupling the pure catalog to the client/RSC render layers and defeating that separation.
- **There is no drift to fix.** Because `APPS` is generated from the registries, the two cannot disagree by construction. A deterministic cross-check confirms the invariant holds today: **122 module meta ids ↔ 122 component bindings, symmetric, zero orphans.** The duplication LP2 feared (two hand-maintained id lists) does not exist — one list is computed from the other.
- **It is a high-risk refactor of a shipped, wired system for no behavioural gain.** LP1→LP6 (the admin rail) and LP7 (template-everywhere) are all live on top of the current direction.

**Consequences.** `docs/LOOM-PLATFORM.md`'s LP2 row is closed as *rejected/superseded by this ADR*. The contract stands: to add an App, add a module/element to its code registry — the catalog, the Loom Apps lane, and the admin rail pick it up automatically; the `APPS` projection is never hand-edited. Anyone tempted to "make Loom the source of truth for code features" should read this ADR first: Loom manages an App's **Layer-2 config/content** (editable data) and **Layer-4 tokens**, never its **Layer-1 function** (the code + its registry entry), which remains git's.

## ADR-502: The unified App contract — one four-layer shape for every functional snippet (LP1)

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-02). Shipped as `lib/apps/*` (LP1, docs/LOOM-PLATFORM.md §3). Records the decision the App-contract files previously mis-cited as "ADR-498" (which is Gift Gems); the citations are corrected to this ADR.

**Context.** Editor modules, page widgets, admin-rail cards, and code-drawn elements had each grown their own registry, gate check, and metadata shape. The Loom Platform needs ONE contract so every functional snippet can be cataloged, gated, placed, and styled uniformly — and so The Loom can index them the same way it indexes images. Two capability systems already existed and had to be bridged, not replaced: community `resolveCapabilities` (`lib/core/capabilities.ts`) and per-Space `spaceFunctionAccess` (`lib/spaces/functions.ts`).

**Decision.** Define the **App** as a four-layer contract (`lib/apps/types.ts`), and derive the catalog from the existing code registries (see ADR-501 for the derive-direction):
- **Layer 1 — Function** (git, read-only): the code + its registry entry. The source of truth.
- **Layer 2 — Global config** (Loom, editable data): operator-editable fields/connections on a `library_assets kind='app'` row.
- **Layer 3 — Instances** (per-surface placements): `app_instances` (ADR/migration LP3).
- **Layer 4 — Style** (per-theme tokens): DAWN tokens only, never stored geometry.
- **One bridged gate** — `AppGate` is a discriminated union over `{none | capability | spaceFunction | staff}`; `appGatePasses` (`lib/apps/access.ts`, PURE) is the single resolver both capability systems flow through, so an App is filtered/locked identically wherever it appears.

**Consequences.** `lib/apps/{types,access,catalog,adapters,bindings,for-scope,app-registry,overrides}.ts` are the contract. Capabilities remain UX on the client and law on the server: every App action re-checks its gate server-side regardless of whether the client showed it. Adding a functional snippet means adding it to its code registry; the catalog, the Loom Apps lane (ADR-504), and the admin bar (ADR-503) pick it up automatically.

## ADR-503: The standardized admin bar — one site-wide, role-resolved settings menu (LP4)

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-02). Shipped as `lib/apps/for-scope.ts` + `components/layout/admin-bar/*` + `components/layout/settings-panel.tsx` (LP4 + admin-rail Phases 1–7, docs/ADMIN-RAIL.md). Records the decision those files previously mis-cited as "ADR-501" (which is the catalog-derives decision); citations are corrected to this ADR.

**Context.** Editing surfaces across circles, events, spaces, hubs, nexuses, practices, and personal settings each had a bespoke edit affordance. The owner's requirement: ONE settings menu for the whole site, baked into the theme chrome, whose *content* is role-resolved Apps from the catalog — personal settings for everyone, the 9-category management spine for editors, operator tools for staff. Nothing hardcoded.

**Decision.** Resolve the bar's content from `appsForScope(scope, viewer, kind?)` over the ONE App catalog. Presence is unconditional for an authed viewer (the personal "You" set is never empty); the management spine appears per `(scope × role × capability)`. `showsAdminBar` is **editor-only** (`appsForScope(scope, viewer, 'editor').length > 0`) so page blocks don't falsely light the bar. The spine order is fixed (muscle memory); the rail is browse-first with a fuzzy search overlay; exactly two levels (category → detail), anything deeper routes to a Focus page; filter-before-render (hide never-eligible, lock-with-reason for attainable). One component renders it as a desktop slide-over and a mobile bottom sheet. Per-scope operator overrides are fail-safe to catalog defaults (`app_overrides`, admin-rail Phase 6).

**Consequences.** `lib/layout/page-chrome.ts` registers the rail once (`global`/`scoped`/`none`); pages never toggle it. Adding a settings surface = adding an App to the catalog, not editing the shell. The bar is theme chrome, tokens only.

## ADR-504: The Loom Apps lane — a read-only index over the code App catalog (LP5b)

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-02). Shipped as `lib/apps/app-registry.tsx` + `components/admin/library/element-preview.tsx` (LP5b, docs/LOOM-PLATFORM.md §4). Records the decision `app-registry.tsx` previously mis-cited as "ADR-500" (which is the Puck template system — the very system §10 requires the lane to stay separate from); the citation is corrected to this ADR.

**Context.** The Loom (the DAM) should present the App catalog as browsable rows the same way it presents code-drawn SVG elements — without becoming a second source of truth for code.

**Decision.** `app-registry.tsx` presents `APPS` as Loom rows read-only (`appsAsLibraryItems()` — pure metadata; `resolveAppPreview()` — a live element render where safe, else a schematic placeholder), mirroring `lib/library/element-registry.tsx` ("code is source of truth, The Loom indexes it read-only"). The preview's live-element path renders through a **client** wrapper (`ElementPreview`) passed down as an RSC slot, because `renderRegistryElement` is a client function and calling it from a Server Component throws (the LP5b crash, fixed here). The lane never edits Layer-1 code — only Layer-2 config/connections/gate, per ADR-502.

**Consequences.** §10 boundary holds: the Loom Apps lane indexes code; it never becomes a Puck-style block editor for an App, and must not be confused with ADR-500 (Puck micro-sites). Pure metadata stays cheap to import (tests load the registry without the render graph).

## ADR-505: The house icon system — Iconify access layer, Lucide primary + Phosphor/Tabler gap-fill, semantic catalog, server-rendered inline SVG

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-02), amended same day (see Update). Phase I (foundation) + the Loom icon lane shipped. Full guide: docs/ICONS.md.

**Update (2026-07-02) — Lucide stays PRIMARY; Iconify fills gaps; NO migration.** The owner decided to keep **Lucide as the primary family** (it is already the site's set, in ~897 files, and they like it) rather than migrate to Phosphor. So the direction is inverted from the original decision below: Lucide is added as an Iconify set too (`@iconify-json/lucide`) and the semantic catalog is **Lucide-first** (`icon('energy') → 'lucide:zap'`, `award → lucide:trophy`); Phosphor (`ph`) + Tabler (`tabler`) are the **gap-fillers** for glyphs Lucide lacks (e.g. `meditation → ph:flower-lotus`, since Lucide has no lotus). The ~897 `lucide-react` files stay AS-IS — there is **no mass migration**. The house `<Icon>` renders any of the three sets by name; client components keep using `lucide-react` directly. The `icon-sets` registry marks Lucide `house`/primary and Phosphor/Tabler `coverage`. Everything below records the original (superseded) Phosphor-house reasoning.

**Context.** The site draws icons from `lucide-react` in ~893 files (268 distinct icons). Lucide is the shadcn default: permissive, tree-shakeable, and fine, but (a) it is the same icon set hundreds of products ship, so it reads generic for a brand-forward product, and (b) it under-covers the spiritual/wellness/energy/award vocabulary that Frequency leans on (Zaps, The Field, practices). The owner wants one coherent house family with distinctive coverage, unlimited modular reach, and the icon sets manageable in The Loom.

**Decision.** Adopt **Iconify as the access layer** and **Phosphor (`ph`) as the house family**, additively.
- **Build-time packages, not the runtime API.** Install `@iconify-json/ph` (house) + `@iconify-json/tabler` (coverage escape hatch) + `@iconify/utils`, NOT `@iconify/react`'s on-demand API loader. This environment is proxied; build-time data is offline, deterministic, and ships no icon fetch to the browser. (`@iconify/react` is installed but unused for now.)
- **A server-rendered inline-SVG primitive** — `components/ui/icon.tsx` `<Icon name="ph:flower-lotus" />` resolves the icon from the installed collection via `@iconify/utils` (`getIconData` + `iconToSVG`) and emits inline `<svg>` with `currentColor`, exactly like Lucide. No client boundary, and the browser only receives the markup for icons actually used. It is **RSC-first**: it statically imports the full `ph` + `tabler` collections, so it must not be imported into a `'use client'` module (that would bundle the collection into client JS). The client-side per-icon tree-shaking path (e.g. `unplugin-icons`) is deferred to the migration phase.
- **A semantic catalog** — `lib/ui/icon-catalog.ts` maps MEANING to name (`icon('energy') → 'ph:lightning-fill'`, `award → ph:trophy`, `meditation → ph:flower-lotus`). Code references icons by meaning, not library, which is what makes the house family swappable and the migration mechanical. A test (`icon-catalog.test.ts`) locks every name against the installed sets so a rename fails loudly, not silently.

**Why Phosphor.** MIT; six weights (thin…fill/duotone) that slot into the DAWN semantic-token hierarchy (thin = ambient, fill = active); and the best coverage of Frequency's wellness/energy/award categories of any general set (`flower-lotus`, `hands-praying`, `lightning-fill`, `flame`, `trophy`).

**Consequences.** Lucide keeps working throughout; the two coexist. New work uses `<Icon>` + the catalog. Migration off Lucide (the 893 files) runs in gated batches via the semantic map, highest-traffic surfaces first, and Lucide is removed only when the last call is gone. **License watch-out:** Iconify sets carry different licenses (MIT/Apache/CC-BY); before the white-label "Frequency for Organizations" product ships, audit exactly which sets were pulled (the Loom icon lane surfaces per-set license for this). `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` in `<Icon>` is safe: its body comes only from the build-time icon data, never from the `name` prop or any caller input (an unknown name renders nothing).
## ADR-506: One Stripe webhook endpoint — consolidate the two routes onto `/api/webhooks/stripe`

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-02). Shipped: `app/api/webhooks/stripe/route.ts` (now dispatches every event type), `app/api/webhooks/stripe/route.test.ts` (member ordering-guard wiring + consolidated-dispatch coverage); deleted `app/api/stripe/webhook/route.ts` + its test. Docs re-pointed (BUILD-LIST step 2, LAUNCH, PRICING, EVENTS-SYSTEM, PATCH-LIST). Meta-scan Phase (Stripe ops).

**Context.** Two Stripe webhook routes existed side by side: `/api/stripe/webhook` (member Crew/Supporter subscriptions, founders grants, Space plan/membership reconcilers, with a `stripe_webhook_events` idempotency claim + ordering guard) and `/api/webhooks/stripe` (Connect `account.updated`, tips, tickets, PWYW supporter contributions, commerce orders, membership dues on `invoice.paid`, ticket/commerce refunds on `charge.refunded`). Both verified with the SAME `STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET` — but Stripe issues a DISTINCT signing secret per configured endpoint, so only one route could ever be the live endpoint. Whichever wasn't configured silently dropped its events: either subscription-cancel / past-due downgrades, or refund capacity-release, depending on which URL the dashboard pointed at. Both `checkout.session.completed` handlers were partial views of the same event. Two options were weighed: (A) consolidate onto one route/one secret; (B) give the second route its own `STRIPE_CONNECT_WEBHOOK_SECRET` and configure two endpoints. The owner chose (A).

**Decision.** Fold every handler into the single canonical endpoint `/api/webhooks/stripe` (matches the `/api/webhooks/*` convention alongside `/api/webhooks/resend`, and is the URL `docs/LAUNCH.md` already advertised). The route: verifies the signature once → claims the `event.id` in `stripe_webhook_events` (idempotency, now protecting ALL handlers, not just the membership path) → dispatches by type. `checkout.session.completed` runs the member/founders/Space-membership branch FIRST (founders grant, or member tier via `apply_membership_event_atomic` with the ADR event-ordering guard, unless it carries a Space `metadata.kind`), then runs the payout-channel recorders (tips, tickets, supporter, commerce) — each already no-ops on a session that isn't its kind, so running all of them for every checkout is safe. `customer.subscription.*` keep the Space-first routing + member reconcile; `account.updated`, `invoice.paid`, `charge.refunded` keep their recorders; unknown types ack 200. On any handler failure the route RELEASES the claim and returns a non-2xx so Stripe redelivers (every write is idempotent). `/api/stripe/webhook` is deleted.

**Consequences.** Exactly one endpoint to configure in the Stripe dashboard, one secret, no split-brain event loss. The idempotency claim now dedups the payout-channel events too (previously they relied solely on per-handler idempotency). **Owner action (unchanged requirement):** point ONE Stripe endpoint at `https://frequencylocal.com/api/webhooks/stripe` and set `STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET` to that endpoint's `whsec_…`; if a second endpoint for the old URL still exists in the dashboard, delete it. Option B (`STRIPE_CONNECT_WEBHOOK_SECRET` + two endpoints) is explicitly rejected: more config surface, more secrets to rotate, no benefit now that one route cleanly handles both concern-sets.

## ADR-507: Security advisor posture — accept the three `extension_in_public` warnings; keep pgvector/PostGIS/pg_trgm in `public`

**Status:** Accepted (2026-07-02). No migration. Records a deliberate NON-change so it is not re-attempted. Meta-scan Phase (security hardening, "A+ across the board").

**Context.** The Supabase security advisor flags three WARN-level `extension_in_public` items: `vector` (pgvector, embeddings), `postgis` (geo), and `pg_trgm` (trigram search). Its generic remediation is "move the extension to another schema (e.g. `extensions`)." The meta-scan brief asked whether to move them for A+.

**Decision.** Do **not** move them. These three are pervasive **type + operator + index** providers, not security-sensitive callable surfaces:
